topic: OpenAI floats 5 percent equity stake to US government
summary: OpenAI is in talks with the US government to give it a 5% ownership stake. The proposed arrangement would involve other leading US AI companies handing over a similar stake. OpenAI has been discussing this possibility with the White House for about a year. A special investment vehicle would be used to hold the equity.

summary_sources.title: OpenAI discusses giving U.S. government 5% stake: Report - The Hindu

sections.paragraph: The US government is taking a keen interest in artificial intelligence, with President Donald Trump planning to meet AI company leaders to discuss potential government investment. This move comes as big tech companies have already spent $155bn on AI this year, outpacing the US government's spending on education, jobs, and social services. Amazon has announced a $50bn investment to expand AI and supercomputing capacity for US government customers, while other tech giants like Microsoft, Google, and Meta are also increasing their AI spending. The investments are expected to accelerate, with hundreds of billions more to be spent in the coming years. The scale of these investments is significant, with some exceeding the European Union's quarterly defense spending. The tech companies' increased spending on AI is driven in part by investor demand, with shares in companies like Microsoft and Google soaring after they announced plans to boost their AI investments.

sources.title: Trump to meet AI leaders over US investment in their companies

Amazon to invest $50bn in AI for US government customers | Business and Economy News | Al Jazeera

sections.paragraph: OpenAI, an artificial intelligence research organization, has experienced significant business growth and investment. In 2026, the company raised $110 billion at a $730 billion valuation, with investors including Amazon, SoftBank, and Nvidia, and later extended this to $120 billion. By April 2026, OpenAI had secured $122 billion in committed capital at a post-money valuation of $852 billion. The company's for-profit structure, established in 2025, allows it to raise investor funds and potentially pursue an initial public offering (IPO), which it filed for in June 2026. OpenAI's ability to attract top AI researchers, despite not initially offering comparable salaries or stock options to tech giants like Google or Facebook, has been driven by its mission and potential. The company's valuation and funding reflect its position as a leader in the development of generative AI models, including the GPT series and ChatGPT.

OpenAI has agreed to changes in its deal with the US military to address concerns over the use of its technology in classified military operations. The company's CEO, Sam Altman, stated that the agreement would explicitly prohibit the use of its systems for spying on Americans. This development comes after a fallout between Anthropic, a rival AI company, and the Department of Defense over concerns about the use of its AI model for mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. OpenAI's initial deal with the Pentagon had raised questions about the use of AI in war and the balance of power between government and private companies. The company has emphasized that its agreement includes assurances against the use of its technology for autonomous killing systems or mass surveillance, although some observers have expressed skepticism about the terms of the deal.

sources.title: OpenAI changes deal with US military after backlash
OpenAI amends Pentagon deal as Sam Altman admits it looks ‘sloppy’ | OpenAI | The Guardian
OpenAI wins $200m contract with US military for ‘warfighting’ | OpenAI | The Guardian
OpenAI to work with Pentagon after Anthropic dropped by Trump over company’s ethics concerns | OpenAI | The Guardian

sections.paragraph: The US government's potential stake in AI companies has sparked discussion about the economic implications. OpenAI has reportedly discussed giving the US government a 5% ownership stake, with CEO Sam Altman suggesting this could be a way to share the benefits of AI. However, Intel has warned that a similar 10% stake in the company could harm international sales, which accounted for 76% of its revenue in the last fiscal year. The White House has hinted at taking equity stakes in other AI and chip companies, which could include Advanced Micro Devices or Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing. Meanwhile, Anthropic has filed to go public on the US stock market and advocated for a "pause" on AI development, despite the Trump administration's push for AI growth. The administration's plans to take financial stakes in leading AI companies have raised questions about the potential impact on the industry.

triage_reason: This involves a significant shift in AI technology ownership and raises concerns about government control and data privacy.
